entities.description: The first complete print edition of the Royal MS 18 B II Middle English romance: text pp. 1-371, notes and illustrations pp. 373-388, glossary pp. 389-400, and indexes pp. 401-408.
entities.label: A. K. Donald's EETS edition of Melusine (1895)
notes: Part I marks Donald's publication package. The complete romance and colophon are printed before the apparatus.
Only this part was published; the planned continuation concerned fuller critical apparatus and introduction.

dateStatements.label: translation and adaptation
dateStatements.value: after August 1478; before or during the making of Royal MS 18 B II

entities.description: The intellectual Middle English translation and adaptation shared by its continuous Royal manuscript witness and the textually affiliated early print fragments. This abstract layer separates the translated work from any one physical carrier.
entities.label: Anonymous Middle English prose Melusine textual layer
notes: Royal MS 18 B II is the earliest and most complete continuous carrier, but it is not conflated with the intellectual translation itself.
The print-fragment relationship remains tentative because direct abridgment and descent from a separate common exemplar are competing hypotheses.

rights.basis: The anonymous work was created and copied in the fifteenth century.
rights.component: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English translation and adaptation
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editions, digital encodings, or manuscript photographs.

dateStatements.label: catalog date
dateStatements.notes: The British Library supplies the broad range. Watermark comparison supports an 1480s context but does not prove a narrower copying year.

entities.description: The earliest and most complete continuous witness of the anonymous Middle English prose Melusine and Donald's base manuscript.
entities.label: British Library Royal MS 18 B II

physicalDescription.dimensions: 285 x 200 mm; written space approximately 200 x 130 mm
physicalDescription.format: Single-column codex, 37-38 lines per page, Gothic cursive
notes: The work occupies ff. 1r-219v: prologue ff. 1r-3v, translator's estates list at f. 3v, and romance ff. 3v-219v.
The narrative proper ends at f. 217v; concluding paragraphs abridged from French precede the colophon on f. 219v.

rights.basis: The manuscript and anonymous Middle English work date to the fifteenth century.
rights.component: The medieval manuscript text as an underlying historical work

entities.description: Six surviving leaves from an early English printed Melusine, probably associated with Wynkyn de Worde and often dated cautiously to around 1510.
entities.label: Six English Melusine print fragments in Bodleian Vet. A1 d.18
notes: The fragments' textual affiliation with Royal MS 18 B II is strong, but whether they abridge the manuscript version or descend separately from a common exemplar remains unresolved.

entities.description: The French prose print family represented by Adam Steinschaber's Geneva edition of August 1478. Scholarship supports this edition or a very close incunable as the English translator's source at family level.

notes: This entity prevents a false claim that any surviving physical copy, including Mazarine Inc. 1284, was the translator's exemplar.

next_steps: Await the British Library response to the submitted enquiry.
If ordering, specify covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and blanks, and all leaves, with the work mapped to ff. 1r-219v.
Obtain written reuse terms covering repository reference access and any future print-on-demand context before publishing images.
openQuestions.question: Does the British Library hold an unindexed whole-manuscript digital surrogate of Royal MS 18 B II, and what would a new complete color reproduction cost and permit?

next_steps: Retain the family-level Steinschaber 1478 relation unless new stemmatic or codicological evidence identifies a physical exemplar.
Do not substitute the publicly digitized Mazarine copy for an unidentified source copy.
openQuestions.question: Which physical French incunable, if any surviving copy, was used by the anonymous Middle English translator?

next_steps: Preserve both hypotheses and avoid a direct copied-from edge.
Review later stemmatic work and any newly identified fragment leaves.
openQuestions.question: Do the early English printed fragments abridge the Royal MS 18 B II text directly, or do both descend from a separate common exemplar?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections which statement governs a new public redistribution of the encoding.
Preserve attribution and the embedded notice until U-M clarifies the conflict.
openQuestions.question: How does U-M apply its current Public Domain / No Copyright-United States statement to redistribution in light of the restrictive availability notice embedded in the 1997 encoding?

next_steps: Contact licensing@bl.uk with the IIIF manifest URI and intended repository or print-on-demand use.
Retain the manifest's exact non-UK public-domain qualification until written clarification is received.
openQuestions.question: What permission or current provider terms apply to publishing Harley MS 4418 IIIF images in the United Kingdom?

next_steps: Inspect a physical or digitized 1981 copy page-for-page against the 1895 scan.
Until then, retain version_of rather than same_as.
openQuestions.question: Was the 1981 Kraus volume a photographic reprint or a reset edition, and did it introduce any content changes relative to 1895?

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the anonymous Middle English prose translation and adaptation, not Jean d'Arras's French work, Royal MS 18 B II, Donald's edition, or either digital encoding.
Donald prints the complete romance on pp. 1-371. Part I describes an incomplete publication plan, not an incomplete narrative text.
Royal MS 18 B II is the base witness. Harley MS 4418 and Brunet 1854 are supporting French comparison sources, not ancestors in the English manuscript's physical-copy chain.

The current MEDictionary mirror XML is a distinct representation: it has an ETS/EEBO wrapper, generic ID CME00000, current U-M rights wording, CHOICE structures, and different markup from the pinned DLPSTEXTCLASS artifact.
The U-M header says that only correction notes were retained, but direct inspection finds six notes of several kinds; this observed boundary is recorded instead of repeating the narrower claim.
The public Mazarine Inc. 1284 digitization is a complete surrogate of that repository object, but the underlying 1478 copy is itself imperfect.
The Harley MS 4418 ZIP contains all 532 IIIF canvases, including bindings, flyleaves, blanks, and spine. The French work occupies canvases 16-519, labeled f. 1r-f. 251v.
The CME repository Unlicense is limited to contributor-controlled material and does not override U-M, British Library, Internet Archive, Institut de France, or scholarly-publication terms.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is an exact pinned copy of a University of Michigan electronic text made in 1997 from Kraus Reprint's 1981 reissue of Alexander Karley Donald's 1895 EETS edition. Donald transcribed the complete anonymous Middle English prose Melusine from British Library Royal MS 18 B II, while consulting the 1854 Brunet French edition and, at selected points, British Library Harley MS 4418. The U-M encoding represents Donald's complete romance on printed pp. 1-371 and six selected notes, but omits the title page, contents, most notes, glossary, and indexes. Although Donald labeled the volume Part I, the romance itself is complete; the projected later part concerned editorial apparatus rather than a continuation of the narrative. Royal MS 18 B II has no public whole-manuscript facsimile located as of 2026-07-15, but physical access and reproduction can be requested from the British Library. A complete 532-canvas IIIF bundle of the supporting French Harley MS 4418, complete scans of Donald 1895 and Brunet 1854, a complete but imperfect copy of Adam Steinschaber's 1478 French print, and relevant scholarship are cached privately. Scholarship supports a family-level derivation of the English translation from Steinschaber's 1478 text or a very close incunable; it does not identify the Mazarine copy as the translator's physical exemplar.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: After August 1478; Royal MS 18 B II probably copied in the 1480s
catalogSummary.title: Melusine

catalogingSubject.label: Melusine
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English prose translation and adaptation of Jean d’Arras’s French prose Mélusine, preserved continuously in British Library Royal MS 18 B II and printed in full by A. K. Donald in 1895. It is distinct from Jean d’Arras’s French work, Coudrette’s verse Roman de Melusine and its Middle English Romans of Partenay translation, the uncertain c. 1510 printed English fragments, Royal MS 18 B II as a physical witness, Donald’s ‘Part I’ publication package, the 1981 Kraus reprint, the University of Michigan encoding, and repository XML representations.

contentParts.label: Complete Middle English prose Melusine
contentParts.summary: The complete anonymous English prose translation from its prologue through Jean d’Arras’s concluding first-person statement and the Melusyne colophon.

contentParts.label: Prologue
notes: The long heading displayed before the prologue was added to the manuscript in the seventeenth century and is not counted as an original title.
contentParts.summary: The translated prologue invokes the Creator, identifies Jean d’Arras’s patrons and claimed chronicle sources, and dates the French author’s beginning of the work to the Wednesday associated with St Clement’s day in 1387.

contentParts.label: List of the estates of Melusine’s sons

locators.value: within DIV1.2 before the main Elynas narrative

contentParts.summary: A genealogical list naming the realms and lordships held by Melusine and Raymondin’s sons, identified by the British Library catalog as an insertion by the English translator.

contentParts.label: Romance narrative in sixty-two chapters

locators.value: ff. 3v–219v; the romance proper is cataloged from f. 3v, with the principal narrative ending f. 217v and abridged concluding paragraphs through f. 219v

contentParts.summary: The translated romance from its opening meditation on marvels and Melusine’s ancestry through the Lusignan dynasty, eastern campaigns, Melusine’s departure, Geffray’s penance and adventures, the Armenian king’s failed test at Melior’s sparrowhawk castle, and the concluding authorial statement and colophon.

contentParts.label: Six Donald notes retained in the CME representation

locators.value: six BODY NOTE1 elements, IDs note.1–note.6

notes: These notes are part of the represented catalog item but not of the medieval work. The XML header’s claim that only corrections of printing mistakes were retained does not fully describe the six notes actually present.
contentParts.summary: Edition-layer notes on the later heading, a manuscript price annotation, an omission and chapter divisions relative to French versions, a cross-reference, and the disputed concluding date in Brunet and Harley MS 4418.

assertion.notes: The Steinschaber-family source gives a terminus after August 1478. Colquitt’s paper evidence points to an 1480s context for the surviving Royal manuscript, but manuscript copying does not by itself prove the exact translation date; the normalized upper bound follows the current British Library witness range.

dateStatements.display: After August 1478; exact translation date unknown
Royal MS 18 B II, c. 1470–1498; probably the 1480s
notes: The current catalog’s broad c. 1470–1498 range and Colquitt’s narrower 1480s paper-context argument are preserved together rather than silently choosing one.

extent.description: One complete prose romance represented in the pinned XML by one proem and sixty-two numbered chapter DIV1 elements (63 DIV1 total), 266 BODY paragraphs, 64 BODY headings, 371 page breaks spanning Donald pp. 1–371, 435 manuscript-folio milestones from 1b through 219b, and six retained NOTE1 elements. The first manuscript recto is represented before the first 1b milestone. This extent counts the work once; nested prologue, estates list, and chapter aggregate are not additional works.

formStatements.description: Continuous prose romance divided in Donald’s edition and the CME encoding into a proem and sixty-two numbered chapters.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: French quotations in Donald’s retained notes are edition-layer commentary, not a second language of the medieval English work.
The U-M and Donald author labels name Jean d’Arras because he authored the French source; this manifest assigns the anonymous English expression to an anonymous Middle English translator.

Royal MS 18 B II is the earliest and most complete continuous witness, but the survival of printed English fragments means Donald’s historical ‘unique manuscript’ wording should not be expanded into a claim that no other English textual evidence survives.

The current Royal catalog’s c. 1470–1498 range and Colquitt’s probable 1480s context supersede an unqualified repetition of Donald’s ‘englisht about 1500,’ while the older date remains historically important.
The work is complete in Donald’s published Part I; the absent projected continuation concerns publication apparatus, not missing romance narrative.

next_steps: Collate Royal MS 18 B II against every surviving Steinschaber-family incunable, including later editions that reproduce the 1478 text closely.
Treat the cached incomplete Mazarine copy as a family representative and do not promote it to a direct exemplar without discriminating variant evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical French incunable, if any extant copy, did the anonymous Middle English translator use?

next_steps: Collate all six Bodleian fragments and the two reported Bandinel leaves against Royal MS 18 B II and the relevant French incunabula.
Preserve Nolan’s abridgment hypothesis and Meale’s common-exemplar alternatives until the fragmentary evidence permits a firmer stemma.
openQuestions.question: Are the c. 1510 Bodleian Melusyne fragments an abridgment descended from the Royal prose version, a witness to a shared English exemplar, or a separate translation from a common French source?

next_steps: Separate linguistic dating of the translation from codicological dating of Royal MS 18 B II.
Compare the translator’s dialect and lexical habits with securely localized late-fifteenth-century prose while retaining Colquitt’s London paper evidence only at witness level.
openQuestions.question: Can the translation itself be dated or localized more narrowly than after August 1478 in late-fifteenth-century England?

notes: The Royal witness originated in England and scholarship identifies an English translation. Colquitt’s London paper-circulation context is witness-level evidence, not a proven translation location.
The inherited U-M author heading for Jean d’Arras identifies the French source author and must not be treated as authorship of the English translation.

scope.description: Responsibility for the Middle English expression cataloged here

assertion.notes: The British Library catalog identifies the f. 3v estates list as inserted by the translator and the concluding ff. 217v–219v as abridged from the French original.

scope.description: Translator-added estates list and abridged concluding paragraphs within an otherwise close prose translation

notes: Source-work authorship is recorded separately from responsibility for the cataloged Middle English expression.

scope.description: Authorship of the distinct French prose source work translated into Middle English
The 1895 EETS source edition and its 1981 reprint
The edited Middle English text transcribed from Royal MS 18 B II

subjects.label: Representations of Christian-Saracen conflict
notes: The label records the romance’s polemical narrative content and does not endorse its representations.

scope.description: Eastern Mediterranean campaigns and battles involving Melusine’s descendants

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose translation of Jean d’Arras’s Mélusine traces the fairy Melusine’s conditional marriage to Raymondin, her building of Lusignan and establishment of a prodigious dynasty, and the chivalric and crusading careers of their sons across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymondin’s breach of the prohibition against seeing Melusine on Saturdays exposes her serpentine transformation and separates the couple; the romance continues through family catastrophe, penance, dynastic succession, marvels, combat, and the adventures of Melusine’s sisters and descendants.

Royal MS 18 B II preserves the complete continuous English prose romance at ff. 1r–219v. Donald’s 1895 volume labels itself ‘Part I’ because an intended further apparatus volume was never published, but pp. 1–371 contain the full romance; pp. 373–408 contain Donald’s notes, glossary, and indexes. The CME XML encodes the romance text on printed pp. 1–371 and six retained notes, while omitting the title page, table of contents, most notes, glossary, and indexes.

Chapter placement and variant evidence associate the English translation with the text of Adam Steinschaber’s August 1478 Geneva print or an incunable extremely close to it. This is a text-family conclusion, not identification of the anonymous translator’s physical exemplar. The Mazarine Inc 1284 copy cached for comparison is an incomplete extant copy of the 1478 edition, not the proven source book.

notes: The manuscript has no demonstrated original authorial title; this concise discovery title is preferred without importing Donald’s publication-part label.
This distinguishes the anonymous English prose version from Jean d’Arras’s French work and Coudrette’s distinct verse tradition.

‘Part I’ describes an unfinished publication plan, not an incomplete medieval narrative: the published volume contains the complete romance, notes, glossary, and indexes, while the projected additional critical apparatus never appeared.

scope.description: Title of the only published Donald volume, which contains the complete romance text

titles.supplied_by: A. K. Donald’s 1895 edition and its 1981 reprint

notes: This is later witness paratext, not an original medieval title or proof that Jean d’Arras authored the Middle English wording.
titles.supplied_by: a later manuscript hand, dated by Donald and the British Library to the seventeenth century
titles.value: A Chronicle of Melusine in olde Englishe. compyled by Ihon of Arras, and dedicated to the Duke of Berry and Auuergne, and translated (as yt shoulde seeme) out of Frenche into Englishe
Here fynyssheth the noble hystorye of Melusyne

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose translation of Jean d’Arras’s Mélusine traces the fairy Melusine’s conditional marriage to Raymondin, her building of Lusignan and establishment of a prodigious dynasty, and the chivalric and crusading careers of their sons across Europe and the eastern Mediterranean. Raymondin’s breach of the prohibition against seeing Melusine on Saturdays exposes her serpentine transformation and separates the couple; the romance continues through family catastrophe, penance, dynastic succession, marvels, combat, and the adventures of Melusine’s sisters and descendants.
